question_text: A vernier caliper has a least count (smallest division) equal to 0.1 mm. The commonly accepted estimate of absolute uncertainty for a single measurement using this instrument is:
answer: Standard practice is to take half the least count as the absolute uncertainty: 0.1 mm / 2 = 0.05 mm, so ±0.05 mm.
explanation: The least count (LC) of an instrument is the smallest reading increment it can resolve. For a single manual measurement, random placement and reading uncertainties are typically estimated as ±(LC/2). Here LC = 0.1 mm, so the absolute uncertainty is ±0.1/2 = ±0.05 mm. Option A (±0.1 mm) corresponds to using the full least count instead of half — a common but conservative mistake. Option C (±0.01 mm) is too small and implies ten times better resolution than the instrument provides. Option D (±0.2 mm) is twice the LC and is an overestimation. Therefore only option B is correct.
more_explanation: Taking half the least count as the uncertainty reflects the fact that a true value can reasonably lie anywhere within ±half a division from the observed mark due to reading limitations. This convention is widely used for basic error estimation in introductory experiments.
concept_tested: Absolute uncertainty (instrument least count) and basic error estimation
